title: Limp vellum binding, the flexible medieval book cover
subtitle: A wrapper of dampened vellum sewn directly to the text block, without boards
excerpt: The limp vellum binding predates the cased book by more than a millennium. A dampened vellum wrapper is laced or sewn to the text block without rigid boards, producing a light, flexible book that closes with twisted cord ties. Medieval account books, breviary texts, and notarial manuscripts used this structure because it was cheap to produce and easy to repair.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on early binding structures and limp-cover forms. Cyril Davenport, The Book, Its History and Development (1907), chapters on early European bindings.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bookbinding structure without rigid boards. The cover wraps around the text block and is secured by lacing or sewing, leaving the book flexible along its full height.
The method of attaching covering material to a text block by threading the sewing cords or tapes through holes in the cover.
Twisted thong or cord attachments at the foredge and sometimes at head and tail that close a limp binding in the absence of a clasp.

content.text: A
limp binding
has no boards. The vellum wrapper folds around the text block and is held by
lacing
the sewing cords through pierced holes in the vellum itself.
Cord ties

at the foredge close the book in the absence of a rigid clasp. Medieval documentary manuscripts, portable breviaries, and notarial registers all used this structure, which Cockerell describes as 'the original form of all our modern bindings.'

attrs.heading: Cutting tools safety
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.

content.text: What you need
For the vellum wrapper: a single piece 80-100 mm wider and 20 mm taller than the open text block. For the cord ties: alum-tawed leather thong or twisted linen cord, 300 mm per tie.
Sewing the text block

Sew the text block on two or three pairs of linen tapes using a supported long-stitch or kettle-stitch sewing. The tapes must protrude beyond the head and tail of each spine edge by at least 30 mm. These protruding ends will lace through the vellum wrapper.
Preparing the vellum wrapper
Cut the vellum to size with a craft knife against a metal ruler. Dampen the flesh side lightly with a damp sponge.
Mark the spine width on the wrapper: score a vertical fold line at the spine width from each edge. Score both fold lines lightly with the bone folder against the ruler before the vellum dries.
At each tape position, pierce paired holes through the vellum wrapper on the front board zone and the back board zone. These holes receive the protruding tape ends from the sewn text block.
Lacing and tying

Place the sewn text block inside the opened vellum wrapper, spine against the scored fold line. Lace the tape ends through the pierced holes: in through the outer hole, across the inner face of the wrapper, out through the inner hole. Paste the tape end flat against the wrapper's inner face.

At the foredge, pierce two holes through the wrapper on each board zone for the cord ties. Thread and knot the cord ties so that when looped around the foredge and tied, the two boards are pulled snug against the text block.
Press the assembled book under a flat board and weights for several hours while the vellum dries. The wrapper will pull slightly taut and assume the shape of the text block.
Common problems

items.symptom: The wrapper cockles as it dries
items.cause: The vellum was too wet when wrapped or dried unevenly.
items.fix: Press firmly under boards immediately after wrapping. Uneven drying in a warm room can be managed by lightly re-dampening the outer face with a clean sponge to balance moisture.

items.symptom: The lacing tape ends pull out of the holes
items.cause: The holes were too large or the tape was not adhered.
items.fix: Pass the tape end through twice and paste it with a small patch of Japanese tissue over the inner face.
